coredata.dc:title: Structure and properties of biomedical Co–Cr–Ti alloys

coredata.dc:creator: Matković, Tanja
Slokar, Ljerka
Matković, Prosper
coredata.dc:description: In this work 12 dental Co–Cr casting alloys containing titanium in the range of 4–12wt.% have been studied. Optical and SEM microscopy show that all samples have similar two phases dendritic microstructure. By means of X-ray diffraction two phases of similar compositions were identified as fcc and hcp Co-rich solid solutions. Hardness measurement reveals that hardness increases with titanium content and the percentage of hardener phase. The corrosion resistance of most as-cast alloys is prominent thanks to the presence of titanium. According to the microstructure, the corrosion resistance and the criterion that the hardness should be close to that of commercial Co–Cr base dental alloys, samples 3–5 can be excellent candidates for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co–Cr–Ti alloys
Dental alloys
Microstructure
Hardness
Corrosion resistance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical micrograph of the as-cast Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy with two phases dendritic solidification microstructure.

SEM micrograph of the as-cast Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy and profile concentration (PK) along AA′ line for Co, Cr and Ti (a) secondary electrons (SEI) 3000×, (b) PK for sample with etching and (c) PK for sample without etching.
Selected region of XRD patterns of Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy related to the different heat treatments.
Anodic polarization curve of Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy examined in 0.9% NaCl at 20°C.
Chemical composition and Vickers hardness of the as-cast and heat treated Co–Cr–Ti alloys (30N, 10s)
Chemical composition and microhardness (0.49N, 10s) of dendrites and interdendritic region for Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy
The results of quantitative metallography and hardness of Co61Cr35Ti4 alloy for the different heat treatments
Electrode potential E pitt (vs. SCE) for Co–Cr–Ti alloys

article.ce:title: Structure and properties of biomedical Co–Cr–Ti alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Metallurgy, University of Zagreb, Aleja narodnih heroja 3, 44000 Sisak, Croatia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work 12 dental Co–Cr casting alloys containing titanium in the range of 4–12wt.% have been studied. Optical and SEM microscopy show that all samples have similar two phases dendritic microstructure. By means of X-ray diffraction two phases of similar compositions were identified as fcc and hcp Co-rich solid solutions. Hardness measurement reveals that hardness increases with titanium content and the percentage of hardener phase. The corrosion resistance of most as-cast alloys is prominent thanks to the presence of titanium. According to the microstructure, the corrosion resistance and the criterion that the hardness should be close to that of commercial Co–Cr base dental alloys, samples 3–5 can be excellent candidates for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt–chromium base alloys can be generally described as non-magnetic, wear, corrosion and heat resistant. Many properties of the alloys originate from the crystallographic nature of cobalt, the solid solution strengthening effect of chromium and alloying elements, the formation of extremely hard carbides and the corrosion resistance imparted by chromium [1]. These alloys have been used for many years as dental and surgical implants due to their excellent resistance to body fluids and biocompatibility [2,3]. The first medical use of Co–Cr base alloys was in the cast of dental implants. In order to achieve better performances and to improve their relatively poor ductility the addition of different alloying elements has been intensively studied [4]. Recently, it is also interesting to develop Co–Cr base alloys suitable for coating with ceramics, or so called enamelling alloys [5,6].

In the past 2 decades, titanium and its alloys are the most commonly used dental and orthopaedic implant materials because of their outstanding properties. Titanium has many exceptional advantages, such as light weight, adequate strength, good corrosion resistance and excellent biocompatibility [7,8]. Therefore, it may be expected positive alloying effects of titanium on the biomedical and mechanical properties of the base alloy. The available data confirm that the addition of titanium to the traditional Co–Cr dental alloy can improve some physical properties [9,10], but also show insufficiency in the investigation of this topic.

The purpose of this article is to present the results of an experimental study of the effect of titanium addition on the microstructure and some properties of Co–Cr alloys. Hardness and corrosion behaviour of the as-cast Co–Cr–Ti alloys would be evaluated with a hope of developing an alloy suitable for biomedical application.

Twelve samples of Co–Cr–Ti alloys with titanium content in the range of 4–12at.% were prepared by melting the elements (with purity better than 99.9%) in a laboratory argon arc-furnace. To ensure homogeneity, the buttons (5g) were three times remelted. Casting of alloys was realised in the same equipment by means of specially constructed copper anode, which has served as the casting mould. In this way, cylindrical samples with dimensions about 7mm×12mm were produced directly.

Microstructural observation of the as-cast specimens was performed by an optical microscope and a scanning electron microanalyser (SEM) of type Jeol, JXA 50A. Samples were prepared using a standard metallographic technique of grinding with SiC abrasive paper and finally polishing with Al2O3 suspension. Etching was made with a freshly prepared solution of 50ml HCl, 5ml HNO3 and 50ml H2O warmed on 50°C, or with Krolls reagent (1–3ml 40% HF, 2–6ml HNO3 conc., 100ml H2O). For quantitative optical metallography, corresponding programmes (Olympus, Image Tool) were used.

X-ray diffraction analysis was performed on polished discs surfaces in the range from 10° to 100° of 2θ value using Cu Kα radiation (Philips, PW 3710). Phase identification was carried out by the matching of each characteristic peak with the JCPDS file [11]. Heat treatment was performed on the sample discs approximately 5mm thick, which were cut off cylindrical samples and heated in a quartz tube filled with argon.
Hardness was measured by Vickers method (30N, 10s). Microhardness measurements were performed on the microhardness tester Leica, PW 3710.

To evaluate the chemical stability of the alloys the method of cyclic potentiodynamic anodic polarization was performed between −600 and +1400mV at a scanning rate of 2mVs−1 in deareated 0.9% NaCl solution at the room temperature. The counter electrode and reference electrode were platinum and saturated calomel electrode (SCE), respectively. The cast disc samples were used as electrodes (working surface cca 34mm2) so that the non-test surfaces of the specimens were coated with epoxy resin. Surface preparation was done by grinding with 600 grit abrasive paper, followed by thorough cleaning in distilled water, degreasing with ethanol and drying. Pitting corrosion was stimulated by polarizing the specimens to noble potential until the passive oxide film on the metal surface breaks down. The parameter of interest is the pitting potential, E pitt, i.e. the potential of passive film breakdown.

The effect of titanium addition on the microstructure and properties of Co–Cr alloys was studied on 12 experimental samples with the composition close to that of the similar commercial as-cast dental alloys (Table 1 ).

Observing the microstructure of as-cast Co–Cr-–Ti alloys under the optical and scanning electron microanalyser, after chemical etching, show that all samples have similar two phases eutectic dendritic microstructure. It consists of dendrites—dark phase and interdendritic region—light phase, as can be seen on the optical and SEM micrographs of sample 4 (Figs. 1 and 2a ), which is selected as representative.

The chemical composition of these two phases were established by SEM and EDX point analyses. The correct average concentration of elements was obtained by using of BE-image (back-scattered electron image) on the sample without etching. Namely, the selective chemical etching leads to an enrichment or impoverishment of elements on the sample surface and to the probably wrong conclusions about the chemical composition of the phases, as in Refs. [10,12]. Evidence of this are the profile concentrations (PK) of elements Co, Cr and Ti along AA′ line for the same sample (No. 4) with and without etching (Fig. 2 b and c). The results from Table 2 confirm that chemical compositions of dendrites and interdendritic region are very close for the sample without etching.

X-ray diffraction analysis was performed on the representative alloy Co61Cr35Ti4 in as-cast and heat-treated conditions. Fig. 3 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns related to the different heat treatments. As can be seen the as-cast sample consists of two phases identified as the Co-rich solid solution having fcc and hcp lattice. After the homogenisation treatments at 1000 and 800°C for 10h and quenching in water, the XRD patterns show also the presence of the fcc and hcp Co-rich solid solutions. The relative intensities of the X-ray diffraction peaks corresponding to the hcp phase increase, while those associated with the fcc phase decrease indicating that a phase transformation has taken place. This is in agreement with the data about the allotropic transformation of metastable fcc to hcp phase in a Co–Cr base alloys [13]. The corresponding amounts of fcc phase were calculated as the rest from the integrated intensities of the (200)fcc and (10
1 ¯
1)hcp peaks using relationship proposed by Sage and Guillaud [14]:
wt . %

1

1 ¯

+ 1.5 ×
I

0
0
.
Fig. 3 illustrated the amounts of non-transformed fcc phase for the as-cast and heat-treated conditions. The XRD pattern for the alloy treated at 400°C for 10h and quenched in water does not exhibit the fcc diffraction peaks. It shows the presence of the hcp phase and the intermetallic compound Co–Cr or σ-phase. The appearance of σ-phase is expected, because it is known that in the carbon-free systems in addition to the Co-rich solid solution, the σ-phase also occurs [15].

The results of hardness measurement on the as-cast and heat treated samples (Table 1) revealed the strong influence of alloy chemistry on hardness values. The titanium content is more important than the chromium content, so that hardness increases about three times from samples 1–12. Contrary to this, hardness of alloys is not significantly influenced by heat treatment, as show its slightly increased values. From the results of the microhardness measurement on sample 4 (Table 2) it can be seen that interdendritic region has a greater microhardness than the dendrites. The reason for this is the different crystal structures of these two phases, considering that their chemical compositions are nearly the same. According to this, the hardness of sample 4 (Table 3 ) increases with the percentage of hardener phase, i.e. interdendritic region after different heat treatments, which are given in Fig. 3.

The resistance of as-cast alloys to localized attack was determined from cyclic anodic polarization tests in 0.9% NaCl solution. With this technique, through a potential cycle the passive, spontaneously formed surface oxide film breakdowns This is marked by a large and generally increasing polarizing current, due to the localized or pitting-type corrosion, as can be seen on the registered cyclic voltammogram of representative sample (Fig. 4 ). Since a material with higher pitting potential can be considered as corrosion resistant, E pitt was used as the criterion for corrosion resistance of alloys. The values determined from the curves of cyclic anodic polarization are shown in Table 4 . From these data, it can be seen that almost all as-cast Co–Cr–Ti alloys show similar and relatively high E pitt values, which are not significantly affected by the alloy composition. Prominent corrosion characteristics of these alloys can be therefore ascribed to the presence of titanium, as one of the most corrosion-resistant material used for biomedical applications. That also confirms our previous investigation of as-cast Co–Cr–Mo and Co–Cr–Ni alloys [16], in them these alloys show less E pitt values than Co–Cr–Ti alloys in spite of similarity of compositions.

The results of the present article suggest, although most of the 12 experimental alloys reveal the appropriate microstructure and corrosion resistance, that samples 3–5 closely satisfied also the hardness values of commercial Co–Cr base dental alloys (∼350HV) and can be excellent candidates for biomedical applications.

Investigation of cobalt–chromium alloys with addition of titanium in the range of 4–12wt.% revealed the following results:
•
Optical microscopy, SEM and EDX point analyses show that all as-cast samples have similar, two phases dendritic solidification microstructure, which consists of dendrites and interdendritic region with nearly the same chemical composition.
•
The XRD analysis carried out on the as-cast alloy Co61Cr35Ti4 reveals the fcc and hcp Co-rich solid solution. The allotropic transformation of metastable fcc to the hcp phase proceedes with the heat treatment at 1000 and 800°C and consequently to this decreases the percentage of the fcc phase. After the heat treatment at 400°C, in addition to the hcp solid solution the diffractogram displays the σ-phase.
•
Hardness values are strongly influenced by alloy composition, so that hardness increases primary with titanium content and than with chromium content. They are also dependent upon the percentage of hardener phase, i.e. the interdendritic region.
•
Most of experimental alloys reveal very good corrosion characteristics, because of the presence of titanium, known for excellent corrosion resistance.
•
According to the microstructure, corrosion resistance and hardness values the samples 3–5 have great potential in biomedical applications.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials Science and Technology, Medical and Dental Materials

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Powder Diffraction File Search Manual, JCPDS International Centre for Diffraction Data, Swarthmore, 1982.
